title: Determine absolute or conditional convergence
description: Determine whether a given series converges absolutely, conditionally, or not at all.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n+1}}\frac{n}{n^2+1}}$  

**Does the series converge conditionally, converge absolutely, or diverge?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The given series meets both conditions of the Alternating Series Test since $~~\displaystyle\lim\limits_{n\rightarrow\infty}\,\frac n{n^2+1}=0~~$ and $~~\dfrac{n+1}{(n+1)^2+1}< \dfrac n{n^2+1}\,$; note that $~f(n)=\dfrac n{n^2+1}~$ is a decreasing function for $~n\ge1\,$.  Thus the series converges.

However, $~~\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^\infty \,\frac{n}{n^2+1}~$ does not converge, which can be seen by using the Limit Comparison Test with $~~\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^\infty\, \frac{1}{n}\,$. 

Hence the given series converges conditionally.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n-1}}4^{1/n}}$  

**Does the series converge conditionally, converge absolutely, or diverge?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Using the standard notation of the Alternating Series Test, in this example we have $~\displaystyle{{a}_{n}}=4^{1/n}$.   

Since $\displaystyle\lim_{n\to \infty }{{a}_{n}}=1\ne 0\,$, the series fails the Alternating Series Test and therefore diverges.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=2}^{\infty }{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}\frac{\sqrt{n}+1}{2n-3}}$  

**Does the series converge conditionally, converge absolutely, or diverge?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
In this example, $\displaystyle{{a}_{n}}=\frac{\sqrt{n}+1}{2n-3}$.  The given series meets both conditions of the Alternating Series Test (the terms approach $~0~$ and the terms in absolute value decrease).

We also know that the **non**-alternating series $~~\displaystyle\sum\limits_2^\infty \frac{\sqrt{n}+1}{2n-3}~$ diverges by a limit comparison with the divergent series $~~\displaystyle\sum\limits_2^\infty \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\,$. 

Hence the given series converges conditionally.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}\frac{\ln n}{n}}$  

**Does the series converge conditionally, converge absolutely, or diverge?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^\infty\,(-1)^n\frac{\ln n}{n}~$ is an alternating series. We have both $~\lim\limits_{n\rightarrow\infty}(-1)^n\dfrac{\ln n}n=0~$ and (for $~n\ge 3\,$), $~\dfrac{ln(n+1)}{n+1}<\dfrac{\ln n}{n}\,$. [This second fact is easiest to prove by showing that $~f(x)=\dfrac{\ln x}x~$ is a decreasing function for $~x\ge e\,$.

Hence, the given series meets both conditions of the Alternating Series Test.

However,  $~\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^\infty \dfrac{\ln n}n~$ diverges, as one sees by noting that   $~\displaystyle \dfrac{\ln n}n>\frac{1}{n}~$ when $~n\ge 3\,$. Since $~\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^\infty \dfrac1n~$ diverges, so does  $~\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^\infty \dfrac{\ln n}n\,$.

Hence the given series converges conditionally.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n+1}}\frac{n}{n+1}}$  

**Does the series converge conditionally, converge absolutely, or diverge?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Using the standard notation of the Alternating Series Test, in  this example we have $~\displaystyle{{a}_{n}}=\frac{n}{n+1}\,$.   The given series fails to meet **both** of the conditions of the Alternating Series Test required for convergence.

i.   $\qquad\displaystyle \lim_{n\to \infty }{{a}_{n}}=1\ne 0\,$.

ii.  $\qquad{{a}_{n+1}}>{{a}_{n}}\,$.

Hence, $~~\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n+1}}\frac{n}{n+1}}~$ diverges.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n-1}}\cdot \frac{2^n}{5\cdot 3^{n+1}}}$  

**Does the series converge conditionally, converge absolutely, or diverge?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Using the standard notation of the Alternating Series Test, in this example we have $~\displaystyle{{a}_{n}}=\frac{2^n}{5\cdot 3^{n+1}}\,$. This meets both conditions of the Alternating Series Test. These terms approach $~0~$ as $~n~$ approaches $~\infty\,$ and they grow increasingly smaller (i.e., $~a_{n+1}<a_n\,$).

Since $~~\displaystyle{{a}_{n}}=\frac{2^n}{5\cdot 3^{n+1}}=\frac{1}{15}\cdot\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^n$, the terms of a convergent geometric series, we conclude the given series is absolutely convergent.

$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{ (-1)^{n+1} n^{-1/3}}=\sum\limits_{n=1}^\infty(-1)^{n+1}\frac1{n^{1/3}}~~$ converges by the alternating series test.

$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{  n^{-1/3}}=\sum\limits_{n=1}^\infty\frac1{n^{1/3}}~~$  diverges since it is a $~p$-series with $~p<1~$ (in this instance, $~p=\frac{1}{3}\,$).

Hence, we know that  $~~\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{ (-1)^{n+1} n^{-\frac{1}{3}}}~~$ converges conditionally.  

Note that in order for us to claim absolute convergence, $~\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{ n^{-1/3}}~$ would have to converge as well.

Both $~~\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{(-1)^n \sin(n+1)}~~$ and $~~\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\sin(n+1)}~~$ diverge by the $~n$th term test. The terms do not approach $~0\,$.

Note that $~~\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{(-1)^n \sin(n+1)}~~$ is **not** an alternating series, in spite of the factor of $~(-1)^n~$ in each term, because $~\sin(n+1)~$ is not always positive or always negative.

$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}}{n}}~$ converges by the alternating series test.

$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{1}{n}}~$  is the divergent harmonic series.

Hence, we know that  $~\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}}{n}}~$ converges conditionally.  

Note that in order for us to claim absolute convergence, $~\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{1}{n}}~$ would have to converge as well.

Both $~~\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }(-1)^n2^{\frac{1}{n}}~~$ and $~~\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }2^{\frac{1}{n}}$ diverge, since $~2^{\frac1n}~$ $-$ the $~n$th root of $~2~$ $-$ approaches $~1\,$, not $~0\,$. The $~n$th Term Test says that if the terms of a series do not approach $~0\,$, then the series diverges.

$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}\ln n}}{n!}}~$  converges by the alternating series test.

$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{{\ln n}}{n!}}~$   converges by the ratio test.
Hence, we know that  $~\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}\ln n}}{n!}}~$  converges absolutely.

$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}}{n^2}}~$ converges by the alternating series test.

$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{1}{n^2}}~$  is a convergent $~p$-series ($~p=2\,$).

Since both the original alternating  series and the series formed by using the absolute values of the terms converge, we know that  $~\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}}{n^2}}~$ converges absolutely.

$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}}{\sqrt{n}}}~$ converges by the alternating series test.

$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}}~$  diverges since it is a $~p$-series with $~p<1~$ (in this instance, $~p=\frac{1}{2}\,$).

Hence, we know that  $~\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}}{\sqrt{n}}}~$ converges conditionally.  

Note that in order for us to claim absolute convergence, $~\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}}~$ would have to converge as well.

Both $~~\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}2^{n+1}}}{n^3}}~~$ and $~~\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{{2^{n+1}}}{n^3}}~~$  diverge by the $~n$th term test, since powers of $~2~$ grow faster than perfect cubes.

Both $~~\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}(n^2+1)}}{n^2}}~~$ and $~~\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{{n^2+1}}{n^2}}~~$    diverge by the $~n$th term test.

The terms of the first series are either greater than $~1~$ or less than $~-1\,$. The terms of the second series are all greater than $~1\,$.
